items.question: How does rule of law typically compare in closed authoritarian systems versus robust democracies?
items.correctAnswer: courts and laws are more easily bent to keep rulers in power
incorrectAnswers: judges are always picked only by lottery
items.explanation: Where checks are weak, legal institutions may serve continuity of the regime rather than neutral justice.

items.question: In an authoritarian system, where is political power usually concentrated?
items.correctAnswer: a small ruling group or single leader with limited political competition
incorrectAnswers: split evenly among many independent courts
items.explanation: Authoritarian rule centers power so opposition and contestation are restricted by design.

items.question: In civic terms, what is a nonviolent tool outside actors sometimes use to pressure authoritarian regimes?
items.correctAnswer: economic sanctions or diplomatic isolation tied to human-rights conditions
incorrectAnswers: ending all foreign travel for citizens
items.explanation: States and organizations may use sanctions and diplomacy to signal disapproval of repression.

items.question: What is a common civic-education point about totalitarian rule compared with milder authoritarianism?
items.correctAnswer: totalitarian systems try to control more of public and private life through ideology and surveillance
incorrectAnswers: totalitarian means no government at all
both terms mean the same thing in every book
items.explanation: Totalitarian aims at broad social control; some authoritarian setups leave more private space.

items.question: What often happens to independent media in strongly authoritarian settings?
items.correctAnswer: they face censorship, closure, or tight state control
incorrectAnswers: they always receive extra public funding
they must be owned only by foreign states
they are banned from reporting weather
items.explanation: Without free expression protections, outlets that criticize power are commonly suppressed.

items.question: Why do scholars say elections alone do not prove a country is democratic?
items.correctAnswer: if opposition cannot compete fairly, the vote may not reflect free choice
incorrectAnswers: because all countries use the same voting age
items.explanation: Elections need contest, freedoms, and fair rules—not just ballots—to support democratic legitimacy.

items.question: What does an uncodified constitution rely on?
items.correctAnswer: a mix of statutes, court decisions, and conventions instead of one single text
incorrectAnswers: only royal decrees with no parliament
rules that cannot be written down
items.explanation: Some countries build constitutional practice from several sources over time.

items.question: What is a bill of rights or rights chapter in many constitutions meant to do?
items.correctAnswer: list protected freedoms and limits on government power
incorrectAnswers: set tax brackets for one year only
name every street in the country
items.explanation: Rights provisions guard individuals and groups from abuse of state power.

items.question: What is a written constitution in comparative terms?
items.correctAnswer: a single formal document (or set) that sets up government structure and powers
incorrectAnswers: only a collection of folk tales
tax rates updated every week by one clerk
rules that apply only to foreign visitors
items.explanation: Codified constitutions organize branches, rights, and amendment rules in one place.

items.question: What is constitutional supremacy?
items.correctAnswer: ordinary laws and government actions must follow the higher rules of the constitution
incorrectAnswers: local sports leagues override all courts
only treaties from one decade apply
the constitution applies only to diplomats
items.explanation: When constitutions are supreme, conflicting statutes can be struck down or reformed.

items.question: Why do constitutions often need special amendment procedures?
items.correctAnswer: so basic rules cannot be changed as easily as ordinary day-to-day laws
incorrectAnswers: to ban all future court review
to require daily public votes on every line
items.explanation: Supermajorities or referendums protect core arrangements from momentary majorities.

items.question: Why do federal constitutions divide powers between levels of government?
items.correctAnswer: to balance national unity with regional self-rule and prevent over-centralization
items.explanation: Layered authority lets local and national governments each operate within defined spheres.

items.question: In a direct democracy on a large scale, what practical challenge is most often cited?
items.correctAnswer: asking every citizen to vote on every issue is hard to manage
items.explanation: Mass participation on every decision is slow and complex, so many places use representation or mix tools.

items.question: In a representative democracy, who typically makes day-to-day governing decisions on behalf of voters?
items.correctAnswer: elected officials
items.explanation: Voters choose representatives who then debate and pass laws and oversee policy.

items.question: In many democracies, what protects minority groups from simple majority rule in lawmaking?
items.correctAnswer: constitutional rights and independent courts
items.explanation: Written rights and judicial review limit what majorities can do to smaller groups.

items.question: What does competitive multiparty politics usually require for a contest to be meaningful?
items.correctAnswer: more than one party or slate can run and organize legally
incorrectAnswers: only one approved list may appear on the ballot
items.explanation: Real competition means voters can choose among alternatives that can campaign and organize.

items.question: What is one common way democracies hold leaders accountable between elections?
items.correctAnswer: free and competitive elections on a regular schedule
items.explanation: Scheduled elections let voters replace or retain leaders based on performance and platforms.

items.question: Which idea is central to liberal democracy as studied in comparative politics?
items.correctAnswer: protected civil and political freedoms alongside majority rule
items.explanation: Liberal democracy pairs voting with freedoms such as speech, assembly, and fair trials.

items.question: Historically, what distinguished absolute monarchy from constitutional forms?
items.correctAnswer: the monarch claimed broad unchecked authority over law and officials
incorrectAnswers: there was no head of state
judges were always elected by lottery
items.explanation: Absolute rule concentrated power in the crown before modern limits and charters spread.

items.question: In a parliamentary monarchy, who usually runs day-to-day government?
items.correctAnswer: elected ministers led by a prime minister responsible to parliament
incorrectAnswers: the monarch alone with no advisors
items.explanation: The sovereign acts as a unifying symbol while ministers hold executive responsibility.

items.question: In civic comparison, what is a figurehead monarch’s main public role today?
items.correctAnswer: symbolic unity, ceremonial duties, and nonpartisan representation of the state
items.explanation: Modern monarchs often stay above party fights while presidents or premiers lead policy.

items.question: What is a constitutional monarchy?
items.correctAnswer: a system where a monarch’s powers are limited by law and elected institutions
incorrectAnswers: rule by random lottery each year
items.explanation: The crown remains head of state, but real lawmaking sits with parliament and ministers under law.

items.question: What is a key difference between a hereditary monarchy and an elected head of state in a republic?
items.correctAnswer: the office passes by family line in monarchy and by election or appointment rules in a republic
items.explanation: Inheritance versus selection rules define who fills the top symbolic or executive office.

items.question: Why do some monarchies have succession rules fixed in law?
items.correctAnswer: to make orderly transfer of the symbolic role predictable and legitimate
incorrectAnswers: to remove courts from the country
items.explanation: Clear succession avoids civil conflict and defines who represents continuity of the state.

items.question: How does fusion of powers differ from strict separation in a presidential system?
items.correctAnswer: the executive emerges from and answers to the legislature more directly
incorrectAnswers: courts always hire the prime minister
there is never a written constitution
items.explanation: In parliamentary models, executive leadership is tied to legislative majorities rather than fixed terms apart.

items.question: In a typical parliamentary system, who is the head of government in daily policy terms?
items.correctAnswer: the prime minister or premier backed by parliament
incorrectAnswers: a hereditary monarch who drafts all laws alone
the chief justice of the highest court
a directly elected president with no cabinet
items.explanation: The prime minister leads the executive but usually depends on confidence of the legislature.

items.question: In many parliamentary countries with a monarch, what is the usual role of the crown?
items.correctAnswer: largely ceremonial with real power in the elected government
items.explanation: Constitutional monarchy often keeps the sovereign as a symbol while ministers answer to parliament.

items.question: What is a vote of no confidence meant to do?
items.correctAnswer: remove the government or force new elections when parliament withdraws support
incorrectAnswers: appoint judges for life without hearings
abolish the constitution in one step
items.explanation: If the cabinet loses majority support, lawmakers can end that government or trigger an election.

items.question: Where do most money bills and major laws originate in a classic parliamentary setup?
items.correctAnswer: in the legislature, with the cabinet steering proposals through parliament
incorrectAnswers: only in regional monarchies with no vote
only through citizen juries picked by lottery
items.explanation: The cabinet proposes policy, but parliament debates, amends, and passes law.

items.question: Why do parliamentary systems often form coalition governments?
items.correctAnswer: no single party wins a majority of seats, so parties must share power
incorrectAnswers: because only two parties may exist
items.explanation: Proportional representation or split votes can leave several parties needing a joint cabinet.

items.question: In civic terms, what defines a republic compared with a monarchy?
items.correctAnswer: the head of state is not a hereditary monarch, usually an elected or representative office
items.explanation: Republics place sovereignty in the people and public offices rather than inherited crown rule.

items.question: In many republics, what document sets the basic rules for how offices are filled and limited?
items.correctAnswer: a constitution
items.explanation: Constitutions define terms, powers, and succession for public offices in a republic.

items.question: What is a federal republic?
items.correctAnswer: a republic where power is divided between a national government and regional governments
incorrectAnswers: a system with no written constitution
a country ruled only by city councils
items.explanation: Federalism layers elected governments; combined with republicanism it rejects unitary monarchy.

items.question: What is an aristocratic republic in historical comparative study?
items.correctAnswer: a republic where a narrow elite held most political power
incorrectAnswers: any country with a hereditary king
a system with no laws at all
items.explanation: Some early republics combined non-monarchy with limited suffrage and elite dominance.

items.question: Why can a country be both a republic and a democracy?
items.correctAnswer: republic describes no hereditary ruler; democracy describes how people participate in rule
incorrectAnswers: because the words always mean the same thing
items.explanation: One label is about office structure; the other is about popular participation and rights.

items.question: Why do some republics elect a mostly ceremonial president while others give the president real power?
items.correctAnswer: their constitutions divide roles differently between president, cabinet, and parliament
incorrectAnswers: because ceremonial offices are always illegal
because republics never use written law
items.explanation: Design choices create parliamentary, presidential, or mixed republican systems.
